title: Patterns 201 · Deep Dive
scan: What you will learn: eight advanced patterns — Factory, Adapter, Facade, Repository, MVC, Event-Driven, State Machine, and Chain of Responsibility — with specific AEM and Edge Delivery context for each

Why it matters: the difference between a maintainable 18-month-old codebase and an unreadable one is two or three of these patterns applied deliberately at project start, not retrofitted under deadline
Factory and Repository are the two most frequently skipped on AEM projects and most frequently regretted at the 6-month mark — skipping them doesn't save time; it borrows it

Chain of Responsibility is the correct pattern for middleware, multi-step validation, and request processing — teams that substitute nested if-else instead incur compounding debt with every new rule added
Naming patterns consistently in PRs and design docs removes thirty minutes of explanation from every design review

blocks.html: The four patterns in Module 01 are the working language of this course. These eight more are the rest of the architect's vocabulary — the names that appear in design reviews, RFCs and architectural debates. You don't need to use them all; you need to recognise them all.

The most common Facade misuse: wrapping a third-party API to hide its complexity, then leaking that API's error model straight through the Facade. If callers have to catch the underlying library's exception types, the abstraction has failed — the Facade must own its own error contract entirely. Translated errors, translated status codes, translated messages. Nothing from below the line crosses it.

Before

Ad-hoc service calls scattered across 12 components, each making its own fetch with its own error handling, its own retry logic, and its own in-memory caching that may or may not be consistent with the others.

After

Repository pattern centralises all data access behind a single interface. Mock injection becomes a one-line test setup. Caching, retries, and error normalisation are added once — not twelve times — and stay consistent across every caller.

cards.title: Factory
cards.body: One function that builds objects of a known type, so callers never use new directly. Centralises construction logic — defaults, validation, identity.

cards.title: Adapter
cards.body: Translates between two incompatible interfaces — your shape on one side, theirs on the other. Where you put the SAP-to-website, legacy-to-new, SDK-to-our-API translation.

cards.title: Facade
cards.body: One simple entry point hiding a complex subsystem behind it. MarketingService.sendCampaign() instead of ten coordinated API calls.

cards.title: Repository
cards.body: One place to read and write a kind of entity. CustomerRepository, OrderRepository. Callers ask the repo; they never touch the underlying store.

cards.title: MVC
cards.body: Model holds data, View shows it, Controller decides when. The grandparent of Stalwart and of Module 09's three-way split.

cards.title: Event-Driven Architecture
cards.body: Build the system around events, not requests. Producers emit, consumers subscribe — Kafka, AEP, agent systems, workflow engines.

cards.title: State Machine
cards.body: Explicit allowed transitions between states. Draft → Submitted → Approved → Published. Used in CMS, orders, loans, claims, approvals.

cards.title: Chain of Responsibility
cards.body: Work flows through a series of stages, each free to handle, transform, or pass on. Validation → enrichment → approval → publish. Especially useful for AI workflows.

blocks.html: Spot the pattern names in design reviews and you save thirty minutes of explanation. Use them in your own writing and you join a conversation that has been going on for forty years.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Patterns Vocabulary
items: Are patterns named consistently in PRs, design docs and reviews?
Where is a State Machine present but not modelled as one?
Where would a Facade collapse complexity that's leaking into callers?
Is the team trained on the vocabulary, or guessing at names?
